design_philosophy.core_loop: Fight through linear stages of enemy soldiers, collect dropped weapons, reach stage boss, escape base
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Survival against overwhelming numerically superior enemy forces in hostile environments; resource management of limited weapons ammo
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Military commando breaking out of enemy POW camp and fighting through base to escape extraction
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: NES version enforces single-player-only gameplay (arcade had co-op), introduces hand grenades as new mechanic, adds interior rooms with power-ups to encourage exploration within linear structure, adapts controls to NES hardware constraints while maintaining core beat-em-up identity

difficulty.lives_system: Health-based with recovery items available in hidden rooms
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression with no mid-stage checkpoints; player must complete full stage to advance
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited; player can explore huts/rooms to find power-ups (full health recovery, brass knuckles for damage increase, armor for invulnerability to projectiles) but stage layout and enemy placement is fixed

progression.upgrade_system: Temporary power-ups found in huts: full health recovery, brass knuckles (increased punch damage), armor (invulnerability to ranged attacks); weapon pickups (throwing knife, machine gun) from defeated enemies
progression.unlock_mechanism: Completion of previous stage required to advance; hidden level select available via Game Genie codes allowing jump to stages 1-5
replayability_hooks: Four distinct stage environments (POW camp, warehouse, jungle, enemy base) with different enemy types
Hidden power-up rooms throughout stages encourage exploration
Two weapon types with different strategic uses (knife preservation vs. machine gun ammo conservation)
New NES-exclusive enemies and final boss compared to arcade version

world_building_techniques: Cold War setting establishes geopolitical context
Stage progression through military infrastructure (POW camp → warehouse → jungle → enemy base) creates narrative of escape and infiltration
Enemy variety (soldiers, frogmen, shotgun wielders, strongman) suggests regional/specialized unit types
Vehicle encounters (assault helicopter, armored truck, motorcycles) ground combat in military logistics
Final confrontation with enemy general as climactic objective
Extraction chopper escape sequence provides narrative closure

boss_design.philosophy: Environmental/vehicular bosses that require tactical weapon use; boss encounters gate progression through stages
notable_bosses: Stage 1 helicopter - defeated using hand grenades (NES exclusive mechanic)
Stage 4 tank - defeated using hand grenades (NES exclusive mechanic)
Final boss: enemy general (NES version features new boss compared to arcade)

faction_indicators: Player character color coding: blue (player 1) vs. red (player 2) in arcade; single player in NES removes this distinction
Enemy uniform/weapon types distinguish soldier roles (standard soldiers, shotgun wielders, frogmen suggest specialized units)
Military hierarchy implied by final boss being 'enemy general'
danger_signals: Assault helicopter and armored tank represent heavy military response
Increasing enemy density through stage progression suggests escalating alert status
Jungle stage contains frogmen, suggesting amphibious/special forces deployment
Final base stage concentrates all enemy types for final push
environmental_storytelling: POW camp setting (stage 1) establishes player as prisoner breaking out
Jungle environment indicates remote/hostile territory for base location
Enemy base architecture progresses from outdoor to fortified positions
Interior huts/rooms suggest civilian or support infrastructure within military compound

sources_used: Wikipedia - P.O.W.: Prisoners of War (gameplay mechanics, stage structure, NES adaptations, enemy types, boss encounters, narrative context)
The Cutting Room Floor - P.O.W.: Prisoners of War NES (level select mechanics)
